posts.name: BILL D A V I E S
posts.cooked:
I don’t have many bottles pre-2000’s. 1975 Château Nairac right now oldest wine I have on hand, but plan on dabbling in some older ports next year. I was wondering where everyone’s vintage distribution is sitting at generally.

Follow up question which older wine have you tried recently that blew you away?
posts.display_username: BILL D A V I E S

posts.name: Alex Valdes
posts.cooked:
I don’t have many bottles pre-2000’s. 1975 Château Nairac right now oldest wine I have on hand, but plan on dabbling in some older ports next year. I was wondering where everyone’s vintage distribution is sitting at generally.

Follow up question which older wine have you tried recently that blew you away?

I suspect the Madeira holders will win this, but 61 Bordeaux and 67 Yquem are my current oldest.

posts.name: Jeff Stettner
posts.cooked: Two bottles of 1926 Drouhin Corton. Opened one a few years ago… was still wine but hardly delicious. More something to look at than drink.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Oldest all told would be an 1815 Bual, and I have a handful of 19th-century Madeiras and sherries. In dry wines, 1921 Montrose and '24 Haut Brion, then quite a few '28s and '29s. Oldest dry white is 1934 Lafon Charmes I think.

posts.name: J0hn
posts.cooked: 1989 - A spring chicken compared to most of you, I’m sure.

Oldest all told would be an 1815 Bual, and I have a handful of 19th-century Madeiras and sherries. In dry wines, 1921 Montrose and '24 Haut Brion, then quite a few '28s and '29s. Oldest dry white is 1934 Lafon Charmes I think.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Oldest all told would be an 1815 Bual, and I have a handful of 19th-century Madeiras and sherries. In dry wines, 1921 Montrose and '24 Haut Brion, then quite a few '28s and '29s. Oldest dry white is 1934 Lafon Charmes I think.

Wow early 1800s, did you ever try it? I do suspect many old old old bottles of wine that still taste good were slightly fortified for long storage. Did you find the older dry white wine still palatable?

Not yet, but a Madeira will still be going strong!

Oldest “viable” dry, non-fortified whites I have drunk were from the 1920s but one is generally in a different register of flavors.
